perturbation.original_text: Recipient will return any and all tangible Confidential Information provided to it by Discloser and copies thereof, to Discloser within 30 days after Discloser’s written request, provided, howe ver, Recipient shall destroy such information if Discloser elects so.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will return any and all tangible Confidential Information provided to it by Discloser and copies thereof, to Discloser within 90 days after Discloser’s written request, provided, howe ver, Recipient shall destroy such information if Discloser elects so.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return deadline to 90 days could violate data protection laws if the information contains personal data and such retention exceeds what is necessary for the defined purpose.  Many jurisdictions have strict rules on how long personal data can be held.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(e) of GDPR states that personal data shall be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. Keeping confidential information for 90 days after a request, if it includes personal data and exceeds the defined purpose, violates this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (‘accountability’). Suitable Recitals ( 39 ) Principles of Data Processing ( 74 ) Responsibility and Liability of the Controller ← Art. 4 GDPR Art. 6 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the return deadline for confidential information from 30 to 90 days, potentially violating GDPR Article 5(1)(e) concerning storage limitation. The law snippet states that personal data should be 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed.' While the original text allowed a 30-day window, the extended 90-day period could be deemed excessive and non-compliant if it exceeds the necessary retention period for the defined purpose, as suggested by the Perturbation and Law Explanations.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information only to its directors or employees who have a need to know the Confidential Information for the Purpose, provided that such party agrees to be bound by this or comparable agreements to the same extent as Recipient is bound and Recipient agrees to be responsible for any breach by these employees and directors.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information only to its directors or employees who have a need to know the Confidential Information for the Purpose, without requiring such party to agree to be bound by this or comparable agreements. Recipient is not responsible for any breach by these employees and directors.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for employees/directors to be bound by confidentiality agreements and absolving the Recipient of responsibility for their breaches contradicts standard corporate security and data protection practices. It also conflicts with data protection laws that require appropriate safeguards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 28(3) of GDPR mandates that processing by a processor (in this case, the employees/directors) shall be governed by a contract which requires the processor to ensure the security of the personal data. By not requiring employees/directors to be bound by comparable agreements, the Recipient fails to ensure adequate security measures as required by GDPR, particularly if confidential information contains personal data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 28 GDPR – Processor - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Where processing is to be carried out on behalf of a controller, the controller shall use only processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that processing will meet the requirements of this Regulation and ensure the protection of the rights of the data subject. 1 The processor shall not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller. 2 In the case of general written authorisation, the processor shall inform the controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of other processors, thereby giving the controller the opportunity to object to such changes. 1 Processing by a processor shall be governed by a contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law, that is binding on the processor with regard to the controller and that sets out the subject-matter and duration of the processing, the nature and purpose of the processing, the type of personal data and categories of data subjects and the obligations and rights of the controller. 2 That contract or other legal act shall stipulate, in particular, that the processor: processes the personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, including with regard to transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law to which the processor is subject; in such a case, the processor shall inform the controller of that legal requirement before processing, unless that law prohibits such information on important grounds of public interest; ensures that persons authorised to process the personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality; takes all measures required p
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for employees/directors to be bound by confidentiality agreements and absolves the recipient of responsibility for their breaches. This directly contradicts the GDPR, specifically Article 28(3), as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which mandates that processing by a processor (employees/directors) be governed by a contract ensuring data security. Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces this by stating that the contract with the processor must ensure that persons authorized to process personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality. The removal of this requirement creates a clear non-compliance with GDPR, making the modified text unenforceable and constituting a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that Recipient can demonstrate: f) was disclosed to a third party after the period of seven (7) years from the date of the disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that Recipient can demonstrate: f) was disclosed to a third party after the period of one (1) year from the date of the disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year creates a conflict with trade secret laws, which can protect information for much longer periods if it remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage. This change could prematurely release information that is legally protected as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. By limiting the confidentiality period to one year, the agreement effectively nullifies protection for any information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret for a longer period, undermining the protections afforded by the UTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified contract shortens the confidentiality period from seven years to one year. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage, and that limiting the confidentiality period undermines these protections. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this, noting that the change could prematurely release legally protected trade secrets. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the changed text sets a deadline that violates federal or state law by potentially negating trade secret protections.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Recipient may make such disclosure if it is required to do so by law, provided always that Recipient has informed Discloser about such disclosure at least two business days prior to the disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Recipient may make such disclosure if it is required to do so by law, and Recipient has no obligation to inform Discloser about such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the notification requirement contradicts common legal and ethical practices related to mandatory disclosures. In many jurisdictions, providing notice of legally required disclosure is necessary to allow the Discloser to take steps to protect their interests (e.g., seeking a protective order).
perturbation.law_explanation: In some cases, forfeiture laws may require a disclosure of assets to the government. While the law may compel disclosure, a party may have a legal right to contest the forfeiture. Removing the notification clause prevents the other party from contesting the disclosure of information, possibly hindering their legal recourse regarding asset forfeiture. For example, 21 U.S.C. § 853(k) provides third parties with a process to seek amendments to forfeiture orders. The lack of notice prevents them from initiating this action.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the Recipient to inform the Discloser about legally required disclosures. This directly contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation that such notice is crucial for the Discloser to protect their interests, such as contesting asset forfeiture under laws like 21 U.S.C. § 853(k). While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (21 USC 853) focuses on criminal forfeitures and doesn't explicitly mandate notification, the Law Explanation uses it as an example where lack of notice would hinder legal recourse. The original contract provided a safeguard, which the modified contract eliminates, creating a legal contradiction by potentially denying the Discloser their right to legal action.

perturbation.original_text: In addition and regardless of negligence or fault or proof of an actual damage, the parties agree to contractual penalty in the amount of EUR 15,000.00 for the event of a breach of this agreement. Further claims of the injured party remain unaffected.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition and regardless of negligence or fault or proof of an actual damage, the parties agree to contractual penalty in the amount of EUR 1,500,000.00 for the event of a breach of this agreement. Further claims of the injured party remain unaffected.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the contractual penalty to EUR 1,500,000.00 could be considered an unenforceable penalty under Austrian law if it is disproportionate to the actual damages suffered and considered excessive or unconscionable. Austrian courts can reduce or invalidate such penalties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Austrian Civil Code (Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch - ABGB)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1336 of the Austrian Civil Code (ABGB) allows courts to moderate (reduce) contractual penalties if they are excessively high in relation to the damage actually suffered. A penalty of EUR 1,500,000.00 for a breach of a confidentiality agreement could be deemed excessive, especially if the actual damages are significantly lower, making the penalty unenforceable to its full extent.

Der Schuldner erlangt mangels besonderer Vereinbarung nicht das Recht, sich durch Bezahlung des Vergütungsbetrages von der Erfüllung zu befreien. Wurde die Konventionalstrafe für die Nichteinhaltung der Erfüllungszeit oder des Erfüllungsortes versprochen, so kann sie neben der Erfüllung gefordert werden. Die vertragschließenden Teile können eine besondere
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the contractual penalty significantly, potentially contradicting Austrian law regarding excessive penalties. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both state that Section 1336 of the Austrian Civil Code (ABGB) allows courts to moderate penalties if they are excessively high in relation to the damage suffered. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes the text of § 1336 ABGB, which discusses agreements regarding payment in case of non-fulfillment. The increase from EUR 15,000.00 to EUR 1,500,000.00 could be deemed excessive and therefore unenforceable under Austrian law, creating a legal contradiction as the changed text establishes a penalty that may not be legally upheld.
